title: Westpac tips shift in home lending
full_text:
The Albanese government’s budget bombshell has led to Westpac recalibrating its approach to retail banking, with its new chief tipping an explosion in interest-only lending as a response.
Westpac consumer chief executive Carolyn McCann said she expected a major shift in investor appetite for home lending in response to changes to negative gearing and capital gains discounts.
Ahead of giving her first investor update since taking on the job, Ms McCann said the bank was already seeing a 10 per cent drop in home loan applications in the wake of the budget move.
All of this has been driven by investors pulling back from the market, she said. Ms McCann stressed Westpac was still seeing growth from its home lending arm, which had some $529.7bn in mortgages as of December 2025. She said Australia still had several factors that would feed underlying demand for mortgages, with population growth and a national supply shortage key among them.
But Ms McCann, has been part of the Westpac executive team since 2018, declined to offer a view on the tax changes.
“It’s up to the government to make these policy changes, and our view is to work within it,” she said.
That’s despite others in the banking sector, including Commonwealth Bank chief Matt Comyn, lining up in opposition.
Ms McCann said she expected investor borrowing trends to also shift in response to the tax changes, with a rise in interest-only lending where the borrower does not pay down the principal of the loan.
A rapid rise in interest-only lending in 2017 led to the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority putting speed limits on the banking sector over the loans.
Westpac, the second largest home lender in Australia behind CBA, was among the biggest writers of interest-only mortgages at the time. But Westpac, like CBA, is now being nibbled at by competitors including Macquarie, with its share of the mortgage market sagging.
Hot competition in the home-lending market has also driven down margins and returns.
Ms McCann said she planned to turn this around, pointing to work within the bank to retain more customers and win more business.
Changes being driven by Ms McCann include the poaching of Macquarie’s Luis Uguina, the former chief digital officer at the rival lender.
Mr Uguina will appear at Thursday’s market briefing.
Ms McCann said some of the changes already underway at Westpac were tackling how it dealt with its retail banking customers, responding faster to their requests for service.
She said the plan was for Westpac to offer more services to the nearly 6.5 million of its nearly 10 million customers who did not use it as their main bank.
Customers who do have Westpac as their main bank are now highlighted in Westpac’s systems, with the bank marking their accounts with a love-heart emoji, flagging to staff the need to better service these customers.
“We need to be very loyal to them because they’ve been loyal to us. If a customer doesn’t have that love heart, then there’s the opportunity to grow the business we do with them,” Ms McCann said.
Westpac also wants to write more loans directly for its customers, rather than leaning on the broker network to distribute.
Ms McCann said Westpac was already writing some 38 per cent of home loans in-house, but said she hoped this would lift a “couple of percentage points” every six months, with the goal of writing 50 per cent of mortgages internally.
She said Westpac was on the path to this by boosting the turnaround time of its mortgages, as well as making changes in the leadership team of its home loan business.
Alongside this, Westpac has put in place new processes, as well as boosting the pay for its mortgage staff.
Ms McCann said in the past Westpac had built up too many processes in its retail bank that made it hard to on-board customers or keep them.
She pointed to Westpac’s Project Unite, a whole of bank scheme aimed at untangling systems and eliminating duplicated processes, as a key benefit for the retail division.
Westpac chief executive Anthony Miller has pushed the bank on Unite. But Ms McCann stressed Westpac would retain its brands St George and Bank SA, noting some customers were attached to them.
She said this would involve better positioning St George and Bank SA to customers.
“We plan to be a lot more deliberate than we’ve been in the past,” she said. Westpac is also hoping to win more customers by targeting those who enter the country as migrants or international students.
Those arriving from China, India, New Zealand and the UK are key target markets. Westpac has also set up 50 branches as New Arrival hubs, with staff speaking multiple languages aligned to migrant groups.
